## [00:00 - 03:03]

All right, some three, two, one nuts and bolts, things to think about as you play this game. First, it can be played in person, and it's often very fun to play in person. When you're playing with a group of people, you have one person playing through two, one, and the others will offer the prompts. If you're offering prompts and you want to be nice to someone, here are some thoughts. One of the kindest prompts you can ever offer someone is a location. Just say kitchen or front lawn, because all you're asking them to do is find something that ever happened in a kitchen or on the front lawn. It's likely they're going to find a story rather easily. Also choose objects that people touch all the time. So a pen or a phone, if someone wears glasses, give them the word glasses. That's great. And also choose words that have multiple meanings. So a word like bat could be the creature that flies or the object we use to hit a baseball. Those are all very kind words to use, but you don't need to necessarily be kind either. Actually, if you were to give me a word like car, that would be really difficult for me, because so many things happen in a car. For me to find a new story about a car might be challenging, especially for someone like me who tells stories all the time. I once had a group of people give me the words chandelier, fondue pot and potpourri, just trying to like stick me with not being able to find a story. I actually had a chandelier story and I had a fondue pot story and I took the chandelier story to the moth and won a championship with it. And then emailed that class and said, ha, I found a story. So sometimes the more obscure an object is, the more likely you are to recover a story from the past. But if you want to be kind or you're playing with kids, kids love this game because it lowers all the stakes on storytelling. It just invites them to talk about a topic that they get to choose. So if you're playing with someone who's nervous or you're playing with kids, offer those generous words and that'll help a lot. You can also play this game by yourself. I play it all the time by myself. I routinely find myself in meetings that I don't need to be attending, but someone else in the world feels I do need to attend. And in those circumstances, while they're blathering away and nothing they're saying has any meaning to me, I play three, two, one. I open my computer. I go to perchance.org slash object. I put in the number three and I click randomize. And I get three new words. And while something is happening at the front of the room, things that don't mean anything to me, I'm looking at those three words and I'm thinking about my life through the lens of those three words. I'm finding a story, a new story, and then I'm telling it to myself.

## [03:03 - 06:04]

The most important audience for every story you will ever tell is yourself. And then when I find those stories, I add them to my homework for life. That's the same way I'll be adding banana boy to my homework for life today. When we find these stories, when we recover them from the past, they become part of the pile of stories that constitute our lives. So this is a game you can play with people, but just as effectively play it by yourself in those moments when you need to escape those moments when you need to get away from what's happening and you want to do something as valuable as explore your life and pull a memory forward. Also, if you're playing homework for life and sort of someone is spending that one minute thinking about what their story will be, you should be thinking about what your story would have been. When I was thinking of my banana stories, did you find a banana story in your own life? If you did, it belongs in your homework for life. Did you have a sticky note story? If you found one while you were listening to me look for mine, add it to your homework for life. Did you have a whip story? If you did, congratulations, your life is more interesting than mine, add it to your homework for life. Not only is the person who is doing the three to one looking for a story, but the audience should simultaneously be thinking about if I had to tell a story, what would I be looking for? It's a chance for everyone in the room to explore their past and find new stories. My family and I play three, two, one all the time. My kids love it. About once a week at dinner time, they'll say, can we play three, two, one at dinner? And they'll throw words at me and I will tell them stories about my life. And then I throw words at them and they tell stories about their life, stories they would never think to tell. And so I get these tiny little windows into my kids lives, windows I would not otherwise have. A woman who once took my workshop sent me an email about a year later. She told me that she had taught her son how to play three, two, one. And when they're driving around town, the son is sitting in the back seat. And every time they get into the car, he starts shouting words at her. Here's three words, mom. Tell me a story. The woman said, I used to get in the car and we listened to music. It was a lovely drive to school or to the grocery store and we would listen to beautiful music. And now instead I can never turn the radio on because my son is constantly shouting words at me and demanding stories. And she wrote to me and said, it's making me a little crazy. But then she added, I'm so grateful for it because my son is learning things about my

## [06:04 - 07:35]

past that I never would have thought to tell him. He's learning about what I was like as a kid and a teenager and a young adult. My son is learning who I am as a person by sitting in the back seat and shouting out teddy bear, blade of grass, bicycle tire. And while I'm navigating traffic and dealing with avoiding pedestrians, I am searching my life for a story related to one of those three words that I then share with my son, a story I never would have thought of sharing. And she wrote, someday when I'm not here, my son will be filled with stories from my life. And she told me that makes her so happy. So three to one is a strategy that you can use to mine your past, that enormous past of stories that's just waiting to be found. It's also a way to share those stories with the people around you if that's what you'd like to do. And it's a wonderful way to fill your life, to connect the past and the present, to bring all of these things that make up your life, the narrative of your life together. Telling stories that you can tell first to yourself and then maybe to others. That's three to one. Start today. Go play right now. Good luck.
